text:
We have issues with our ice maker in our refrigerator. It is far too slow to produce ice and we have considered getting another refrigerator because of it. We usually just buy bags of ice to solve the problem, but for convenience sake, I decided to get this Frigidaire ice maker.

Compact in size, this ice maker sits on the countertop and produces just enough ice to make it useful for beverages and other modest needs. You just open the flip top, pour in water, and wait about 10 to 15 minutes for the first batch of cubes of ice to be made. The small cubes are bell/gumdrop shaped and are small enough to fit in tight spaces, making them good for those who want to add ice to, say, water bottles and other receptacles that cannot handle normal sized ice cubes.

The outside of this ice maker has lights that indicate Ice Full, Add Water, and Ice Making. The top two indicators display a red light, to get your attention The one annoyance I have with this ice maker is that the ice tends to stack up in the back, which triggers the sensor and causes the ‘Ice Full’ light to come on. There could, in fact, be plenty of space in the ice bin and you will need to open, pull the ice down from the back, and close to make the ice maker resume. The problem is that the ice is dumped in back and stacks up on the back edge. A change to the engineering- having it dump the ice in the middle of the ice container, for example- would fix this problem.

Other than that, this Frigidaire ice maker works well and produces the necessary ice to use in beverages for my family of four. It takes up little space and you can easily flip open the lid to get the ice you need. It’s a good investment for those who have a faulty ice maker in the fridge and who are tired of making regular trips to the store to purchase bags of ice.

text: We’ve owned this refrigerator for 16 months and it is by far the best refrigerator we have ever owned. It does what you would expect from a $3,000 refrigerator like keeping everything cold but we are extremely pleased that it doesn’t have any of the problems we’ve had with other refrigerators like Samsung. It is extremely quiet which is important to us since it’s located in a kitchen/living room combination room—the Samsung we had before was at times so loud we had to turn up the volume on the living room TV. It makes an abundance of ice cubes without freezing them into clumps and jamming the dispenser and it dispenses ice without it flying everywhere. The ice tray is easy to access and remove. The middle drawer is spacious for storing drinks and having ready access to other regular use items like cheese, cold cuts and yogurt. The door-in-door is great for quickly grabbing items without having to open the main compartment—we use it for eggs, butter, creamer, salad dressing and water and wine carafes. The bottom freezer is large with an upper drawer and lower compartment. The French doors and middle and freezer drawers are a bit heavy so it takes a little more than a gentle push to close them but that’s a minor issue considering how great the refrigerator performs.

text:
I purchased this to brew stronger coffee, like most of you. However, it has certainly taken the convenience and ease out of the single cup brewing process....with all the prep and trickery and cleaning and leaking and ...what not.

I am a mechanical design engineer/manufacturing engineer familiar with designing parts from metal and plastic injection molded products, like the gray base and the mating gray screw-on cover.

The problem with any machined or molded parts/assemblies is tolerances, especially with plastic injection-molded products. There is a "recipe" for such molded parts when filling the mold with hot flowing "liquid" plastic quickly....it's time and temperature dependant. Each run of manufacturing the My K Cup can be slightly different in dimensions by a few mere thousandths of an inch...creating a "gap" when the assembly is used. Possibly...

In the My K Cup design, there are multiple interfaces where loose or out-of-spec tolerances can cause a problem...ie, the thickness of the filter basket is dimension x.xxx", and there exists the gap between the gray base and the gray cover of x.xxx" when assembled. if all these dimensions and tolerances do not provide a leak-proof seal, there is a good chance the pressurized water will flow up to the cap and over the rim of the basket filter flange....and taking coffee grinds down into your cup.

I have tried all the solutions listed on so many good reviews (and used some of them below)....however, besides being a chore, I still got a different strength cup of coffee. Today, I finally realized what the solution to this problem is---it's missing a compliant gasket to eliminate any dimensional shortcomings between the dimensions of the flanged threads on the base and the cover, and the thickness of the flange on the filter basket, etc.

so I decided to take some aluminum foil from the roll and create a seal to accommodate for any possible tolerance issues. I pulled out about 4 inches of aluminum foil, and folded it in half. now I had a piece of foil measuring 2 inches by 12 inches long, but 2x thick. I then cut 2"x2" squares to cover just more than the diameter of the filter basket diameter. I also poked a hole in the center for those three fins found on the underside of the gray cover to allow for the hot water to enter the filter basket. Basically, I've replicated the foil found on original K-Cup design, but without the adhesive seal. Guess what? It worked!! Finally, no trimming of old K-Cups, no reusing old K-Cups...which may or may not be a health concern as others have mentioned.

so to make it hopefully more simple to follow, here's what I did, which includes hints and tips from many other commenters.

1) remove the rubber gasket/o-ring from the puncturing needle from the head of the unit, place a #10 stainless steel washer on the post, and carefully place the rubber gasket/o-ring back onto the post in the same orientation as it was before you removed it. This will keep the rubber gasket/o-ring from sliding back up the post.
2) grind coffee or coffee beans to a fine grind. 5-10 seconds in the small coffee bean grinder. too fine will allow fine coffee particles thru the screen...
3) place filter basket into gray base (i used a tall shot glass to hold the gray base during this process)
4) fill the filter basket 75% and lightly tamp down
5) prepare the 2 ply aluminum foil square so it's 2" x 2" square and place atop the filter basket
6) fold aluminum squares around the edge of the basket and gray base
7) while holding aluminum square in place, puncture center of foil with something to create about a 1/4" hole.
8) screw the gray cover onto the base. (Note, this can be tricky if the foil is on the threads between the gray base and gray cover...but it's not a steep learning curve...you'll get the hang of it.)

so having done this, i anxiously pressed start...and FINALLY, I got a good strong cup of coffee with freshly ground Starbucks French Roast! the only grinds in my mug was the too-finely ground coffee. I have to get that grind time down better...or learn to live with leaving 1/8" of basically cold coffee at the end of my cup of morning Joe...

Kerig...perhaps you can add a permanent gasket to the underside of the cover? I would think then you have this common complaint eliminated.

text:
This is one organizational container I didn’t know I wanted until I recently saw it on YouTube.

We originally had a container that had a separate lid on top, but I always had to take the lid off to get to the eggs. Also, it wasn’t useful to store anything on top of the container. I always needed two hands since I had to take the container out of the fridge. I finally stopped using it because it just didn’t seem practical.

This pullout drawer container is the perfect solution for me. The carton of 18 extra-large eggs I bought today fit in this container perfectly. The drawer pulls in and out smoothly.

The plastic it is made from is quite sturdy and BPA-free. The container is easy to take apart and clean.

Some reviewers said there is nothing to catch the drawer and they were afraid they would pull the eggs out accidentally and spill them.

There is a very slight edge on mine, but frankly…I’m pretty careful with my eggs. I think just being aware they are delicate like any time you would handle an egg carton is good enough for me.

I did put little rubber bumpers on the bottom on my container to help keep it from sliding around. I put bumpers on lots of things and always have a supply on hand.

I am thinking that, as I use up my eggs, I may just keep pulling them forward every so often, so I don’t have to pull the drawer out all the way. That should help balance what is stored in there. Also when adding new eggs, they should go in the back.

Still thinking about how to note the expiration date. A dry erase marker might work or a removable label. I guess you can even write one of the eggs with a sharpie.

I love how I can see how many eggs are in there, so I can tell when I am close to running out. Sometimes I buy more eggs when I don’t need them so this should help me keep better track.

I think this is a great value and quality and will be buying a second one so I can stack it on the other one (also with rubber feet). This will help me store eggs more efficiently, since egg cartons often come in different size cartons and brands.

I am very pleased, since I love organizing and love a tidy fridge.

text:
I have done about ten small loads in this machine and am happy with it so far. I previously owned and reviewed the Wonder Wash manual washer and although I was very happy with it for a year, the hand cranking was getting a little difficult for me as was awkwardly having to drain into a bucket due to the side it drained on.

In fact, the Wonder Wash is faster - you only have to crank for a few minutes to get an effective level of cleaning of a much longer cycle on this machine. Wonder Wash uses less water and would appear to be gentler on clothes. It is similar to comparing a front loading machine's action (Wonder Wash) to a top loading machine (Panda Washer).

But again. Panda does the work so you don't have to.

It arrived with no damage. Having to remove the plastic protective film from the top required having to unclip the top handle to get it all out rather than leave pieces of film. Then I saw the drain hose set up that many have written about. Remembering the review where they said the fastening loop was too close to the end of the drain tube I noticed that and moved the fastening loop lower on the tube to keep slack out of it and raise its end higher so water would not slosh out during the wash. And there is the rub - this tube is a rather rigid plastic, a type of plastic I have often had crack on me on other appliances (vacuum, carpet cleaner) at the bend. I would not want to put such stress on the bend tightly all the time so during storage I used a twist tie to have the tube more relaxed but still hanging up. This is really something their design people should take into account and use a more flexible type of plastic or even rubber. Something that can be easily replaced too. I would think if I disassembled I would find it attached to some fitting in a manner that it can be removed, hopefully it is not cemented. I would not want to junk a machine of this cost for a 25 cent plastic hose cracking.

The other issue would be the odd smell. One reviewer had mentioned something smoking with heavy/frequent loads and I don't think mine is anything like that. There is a rather pronounced plasticy smell when it is working, even with cold water so it is not coming from the tub. I initially thought maybe just excess oil around the motor burning off (but it really isn't smoky). Could be motor sits on something that stinks when it gets warm. I don't know, I think it is getting a little better with time. The odor starts almost as soon as the machine does so I really doubt it has anything at all to do with overworking it. If it persists I might try contacting customer service.

So far I have washed almost everything I did in my Wonder Wash except for sheets. I could do one queen fitted or flat in that - summer weight. I would think I could do the same in this. That is really the limit for my countertop spinner too so is fine with me. I have done a pair of jeans without issue with a t-shirt or two in there too. I do 6-9 minute washes because I don't really want to beat up my clothes and they are generally not heavily soiled. I do 2-3 min rinses (two of them) and spray water in as well as it drains with my shower massager. I also fill it with the shower massage, I have not tried the little fill hose so cannot comment on it though I am eying it as an extension for my countertop spinner's drain hose.

I give the Panda Washer a thumbs-up but I have not relegated my Wonder Wash to the garage. And may very well use that for washing my long lace window panels as I think it has a superior gentler washer action. I may use it for some sweaters as well that I don't want agitated. Otherwise I will be using the Panda washer for everything but heavy blankets and flannel sheets so will be seeing very little of the dreaded laundromat!

UPDATE - my original review was rather prophetic and after 22 months of pretty heavy use I encountered leaking at bottom. Upon draining and close inspection and some testing, it appears to be a small slit at the drain hose bend at bottom. Unlike some others may I have not kept the hose pulled up tightly to get a better water level but rather started to use the useless fill hose as an extension. I don't see any way to make a sturdy repair without replacing the hose owing to where the split is located and being subjected to flexing, hot water, detergents, etc. In order to get to the connection of the hose it appears I need to tear apart the machine almost completely, the tub needs to be removed from the base and that involves pulling out/off the shaft for agitator. I decided to use some waterproof tape temporarily and have ordered a different miniwasher.

I got my money's worth out of this I believe. I just don't think the current price and shipping is worth purchasing again. If they replaced that awful hose with a rubber one or maybe even used a nylon or pvc elbow to make repair easier would be a great improvement.

text:
I have my Senseo almost a year (just found out is being recalled - please check yours! [...] ). Anyway before finding out about the recall I started having trouble finding the only coffee pod I like, Sumatra. It was no longer in any grocery or other retail store in area and even Walmart shiptostore no longer carried. Tried every other non-flavored pod I could get my hands on locally and none were acceptable. There are still places on the web but didn't want to spend even more for what is expensive coffee already. Figured I would try this and would use espresso fine grind as recommended since I like a strong cup. Well, I tried both regular espresso grind and a very fine grind. Both expensive enough that it wouldn't be much cheaper than the packaged pods even. Tried one cup (with an empty just water run first to get everything hot), one-cup twice for a mug full, and two cup setting. The coffee varied from mediocre to impossible to drink. For the most part, I get brown water with a foaminess (not foam on top, more a "general" foaminess) You can tell when it is coming out, after first blast, it is hardly colored anymore. And no coffee aroma. The best results are packing as tightly as possible with the finest grind and using one cup setting (running without pod first to heat everything up and having fresh water in it rather than what was sitting in boiler since yesterday).

That result, however drinkable, is not worth the mess. And what a mess. I knew the pods would be a little messy to fill and figured it would be the price to pay for this being cheaper than buying pods on the web. But the mess to empty! Yikes. Now I remember why I got the Senseo in the first place. To not go through the mess of grinds to deal with. And very fine espresso grinds packed tightly into the pods are very messy indeed to clean out. More like dirt. I don't have a garbage disposal so this is awful. Not only do I have the dry grounds mess filling, I have the wet mess disposal to deal with.

For now I have bought the Sumatra on the web. And will use that until the replacement machine comes. Since that machine is black (that is all they are giving in exchange for recalled machines) and mine is red (the other reason I bought it!). I think I will sell the black one unused and get myself a Keurig. Senseo is too much trouble and whether the Sumatra pods have become difficult to find because they are maybe closing up? Or if it is just being phased out, I can't find a coffee I like in their line anymore so why go on with this.

If you are into very watered down coffee, the ecopads are an option for you. To me it is too much mess for nothing.

text:
Review Update March 2021

I love this washer. We use it almost daily in our RV and it hasn't let me down yet. As long as we have sunny weather for drying, I don't need to use a laundromat at all on the road. We're in the RV full time for 18 months on a volunteer project and this washing machine has saved my sanity. Definitely worth the price.

Review Update to 5 stars - Sept 2020

After using this washer several times in our travel trailer on a trip, I'm seriously impressed. I did 3 loads today after one of the dogs got sick on her bedding. We hosed the bed cover off outside and tossed it and another cover plus 2 regular bath towels in the washer. Set the machine to heavy duty with hot water and 45 minutes later everyone was clean. No odors or stains at all. The spin cycle left even the towels 90% dry - we hung everything on our dog fence outside to air dry and the covers were ready to use in a half hour.

This is super quiet and while there is a bit of vibration on spin, its barely noticeable in the trailer. I did 3 loads total today consisting of 2 dog bed covers, 4 towels, 4 tshirts, a dishtowel, one pair of jeans and 3 days of underwear and socks. We're using liquid Arm & Hammer detergent and Snuggle fabric softener just like at home in the washer.

*********** Original Review Below 4 Star now a 5 star **************

I was so disappointed when we bought a used travel trailer and I realized there was no room and no connections for any sort of washer and dryer or even a combo unit inside. Turns out, I can use this washer in the trailer and not sacrifice much. It's tall and narrow rather than short and squat and that means it can fit in a tighter spot than other appliances. For us, it's essentially in a cubby space adjacent to the RV kitchen sink and takes up about 2 feet by 2 feet of floor space. A worthy trade off to have clean underwear on the go.

My unit has a series of small dents on the exterior, but nothing severe or massive. Cosmetic damage only, it still works. It says "11 pounds" load size, but in the real world, it translates to 3 tshirts, 3 pairs of socks and a couple pairs of underwear. Its not meant for real laundry, just small stuff. I've used it once with lightly soiled shirts and it did get them clean. You also want to use less detergent than in a regular washer. We'll definitely be replacing the connection hoses with sturdier ones pretty soon. For our needs in a small travel trailer, I'm happy with this machine.

text:
I looked at probably 20 portable ice makers before choosing this one.
Price was a factor, but so was customer input from reviews. It seemed like many of the other models were the exact same machine with a different company label stuck on them, and their reviews were so varied, it seemed like you either got a lemon or a good one.

Also, all the others opened at the top and vented on the side. For some reason, I thought this one would work better in our area, with the drawer pulling out at the front, and I THOUGHT the vent would be at the side like all the other machines.

My purpose in buying this is not for a bar or camper, like many people. I am partially disabled, and can't get downstairs to the kitchen on any sort of regular basis. I really wanted to be able to have ice when I wanted some, especially with the heat of summer rolling in very, very soon. I don't like room temperature drinks, esp water or tea, so I'd found myself drinking Pepsi almost exclusively, the only thing I could stand at room temp. That needed to stop!

So, I ended up buying this one, but the experience was not a pleasant one. I love my Amazon prime dearly, but they really botched this one. The shipping dates got moved around even after I paid extra for one-day shipping, and ended up with this being delivered on a totally different day, when I was the only one home and could not come to the door. (Note that Amazon did end up giving me a small credit on my acct to try to make up for the shipping problem, even though it did not solve my problem.) I always chat with the UPS guys through the upstairs window, so they left it for me anyway. I knew my son would be home very shortly after, and he was. He brought the box up, and I have to say that I am almost positive I got a customer return. The box looked like it had previous shipping labels peeled off of it. The box was beat up, torn, and had a crushed in spot on the side. Our UPS guys are really great, so I think it was shipped out from Day One looking like that - who in the warehouses think customers really want something that looks that bad?! (And yes, I know this is a product review section, and I can file shipping problems elsewhere, and I will. Just giving folks a heads-up of how it is shipped and what I received.) This is shipped in its original mfg box with tie cords on it, there is no other packaging other than what the mfg sends it out with - no Amazon packaging whatsoever. Strike One.

I opened the box, not sure of what I would find. It only has two big strips of styrofoam on the sides to hold it.
Ours indeed had a dent in the side, and one of the back exhaust fans had cracked plastic. Not a crisis, but at this price, I really do expect a brand new item in great condition! Strike Two.

SMELLS/TASTE: I read a lot of reviews that talk about the plastic smell and/or taste in the cubes, and how it didn't seem to want to fire up well. IF someone reads the directions (not everyone likes to do that!), you'd see it says to make sure the machine sits for at least an hour to allow the coolant to properly settle back into place after you unpack it. This particular model also says to run a bleach solution through it to help clean it. I DO NOT RECOMMEND THAT!! The machine indeed smells heavily of plastic. Anyone who has had a relay switch burn out on an item knows what smell I am talking about - sort of like melting plastic. Ugh. However, having read plenty about portable ice makers before buying one, I knew that either vinegar or lemon juice would also work just fine. I chose to go with one that was ingestible, rather than bleach!! We did the lemon juice, and ran an entire cycle of cubes through it with the lemon/water. (Toss the ice please!) I then ran two loads (all the cubes a full water tank would make) through it of plain water. AFTER those cycles, I wiped it out again and ran regular water through it to try making cubes we'd use. We had no problems at all with any funny tastes in our ice this way. If you have a bad quality of tap water though, I'd suggest using either bottled, filtered or distilled water. It is only natural that your cubes will not taste better than your tap water. Ours is okay, so we're good that way. I would have bought a water filter (like a Brita) if that had not been the case. No lemony taste, no plastic taste, no gross tastes or odors at all. (Remember, I really do NOT recommend using bleach like the mfg suggests. While I understand the germ-killing properties of the bleach, I really don't want to ingest any of it. I would also think it would be much more caustic to the plastic parts and hoses of the machine, wearing them out sooner. Stick with lemon juice.) It did smell like plastic out the exhaust fan for quite a while, but it is fading away.

NOISE: Of course it is going to make noise. This has a compressor, and it will be making some noise. However, I've found that at its noisiest part of the cycle, it is no different than a microwave oven running, sometimes a little quieter.The ice cubes dumping make me jump a little bit, at least at first, but then again I am easily startled. Made the cats jump too! LOL! However, they are pretty much used to it now, even if they eyeball it suspiciously while it is running.

I have to say that I haven't stopped to time an actual cycle to make the ice. I've tried the small, medium and large cubes though. They are all gumdrop-shaped (or like the Dots candy at the movies), with a hole in the middle of the bottom. It didn't seem to take very long though. The small ones were a little too small for my uses, but would be good for smaller drink glasses. I use a [[ASIN:B003X46LHG Bubba Brands Keg 52 Oz Mug Slate Gray]] to hold the ice at the moment, and love it. If you use the medium size, you can get the first batch in around 10 minutes, but the other batches do seem to take longer for some reason. It has also seemed to get "stuck" where it runs and runs, with nothing going on. I turn it off, let it sit a minute and push start again, and off it goes. I am not sure if it is just my machine with the dents from rough handling, or if all of this model is like that.

I was disappointed that this unit does not exhaust on the side, reason being where I planned to locate it, on a table behind my bed! It would vent towards my pillow, which is just not gonna happen. So, I have it turned sideways so it will vent towards the doorway. It makes opening the ice drawer more awkward, and makes me wish I'd tried one of the top-opening models now. There wasn't anything in the photos and such to suggest that this unit would be different than most of the other portable ice makers. It is. I'll post photos at a later date.

This model's book also suggests plugging the machine in to a GFI outlet, or grounded plug that will shut off if it senses dampness. I don't have one in the bedroom, and currently am using a heavy-duty, three-prong extension cord and plugging it in in the bathroom, where we do have a GFI plug. Not ideal, but better than trying to get a special outlet installed in a rented bedroom.

The scoop is VERY tiny. It works best to scoop towards you, so you don't send ice cubes back into the machine.
The measuring cup works well for pouring, because you have a small reservoir to fill, and you have to go over a small clear rubber "wall" that helps to hold the drawer in place and hold temperatures.

Any unused ice does indeed melt back into the reservoir and is recycled back into cubes. Once I have enough ice for anyone who wants it, I turn it off rather than let it continually cycle. I also unplug it in the evenings when I go to bed for the night, since I'm the only one who can't go downstairs for ice if they want it. I hope that saves on the "vampire electricity" of the unit. Note that this is not a refrigerated ice maker, and the unused ice WILL melt. Many people complained about ice cubes stuck together - I've only experienced that when getting ice that has been done for an hour or so (it keeps the ice for at least an hour after the last cycle at this point) - otherwise, the cubes are all loose and easy to use. If you try to take the ice drawer all the way out after leaving the ice sit for a while, it WILL leak water on you - the water is simply the ice melting back and going through the drain holes that let it run back into the reservoir. (Nope - I didn't even think about the drainage holes when I pulled the drawer out the first time, and had water on the table and the floor. That was my fault though.)

For now, I am mostly happy with it. I cannot tell you how nice it is to have ice when I want it, instead of waiting for someone to get home in the evening and get me some! It was an expensive luxury though, and one I really thought long and hard about getting since I can no longer work. I'm thinking I really want to keep it. The cosmetic issues were VERY annoying, since I paid for a new machine, not a used one, but in this case, I blame Amazon for sending this out with a crushed box from the get-go. I would not have accepted it, had I been able to get to the door and saw it. Again - for some reason, this was the most problematic purchase I've ever had in all my time on Amazon.

This machine has not reached Strike Three yet, but you can be sure I'll come back and update my review should that happen. For now, I'll keep running it, and see how it seems to work, dents, cracked exhaust grid and all. (The dents are even more annoying, because it has a stainless steel appearance. It shows readily. It was more of a hassle to return it than to just keep it, as long as it continues to work.)

UPDATE June 29, 2012:
Well, I've had this machine for a little over a month now. Is it perfect? Nope. Does it make ice?
Yep. Does it make a little noise? Yep, but not unbearable.
So far, the broken parts do not seem to be affecting the basic function of the machine.
While I still have not literally timed the cube cycle (I have good intentions, but seems like there's always
something better to do than watch the clock for ice cubes to drop! Ha!), I have found the medium cubes to be the most useful. In fact, I often use an empty Sobe Lifewater bottle to make quick tea (loving the new tea from Lipton with the honey in it), and the medium cubes fit in this bottle perfectly. So, that might give folks a better idea about the size of the medium cube. The difference in the sizes are basically more (or less) layers of ice on the cubes. The small ones are still too big for a basic 16.9 oz water bottle, and melt too fast when I shake up my tea.
The first batch of the day takes around 10 minutes, but for some reason, they are skinnier than the normal mediums. The machine makes additional batches inbetween 10-15 minutes. Around 2 or 2-1/2 of the water cup it comes with to fill the reservoir to maximum. That will fill the ice bin to the top with medium cubes.

At this point, I increased my stars by one, because of the function of the machine, and it being willing to
do what I "hired" it to do - keep me in cold drinks. Strike three has not yet happened, and I hope it doesn't!
Enjoy!
product: Breville BES870BSXL Barista Express Espresso Machine

text:
I purchased two of these to go with my new Keurig Platinum single serve coffee maker. My favorite pre-Keurig coffee isn't available in K-cups, so I had to find something that would work with my own ground coffee.

Read the reviews of the Ekobrew, had a chance to try one at my brother's over Christmas, and decided this was the one I wanted.

I'm not disappointed. So far, it's working great. I found that I need to grind the coffee a bit finer than usual (I use a burr grinder) to get the right strength, but once I'd figured that out, it makes a great cup of coffee.

Cleaning hasn't been as big an issue for me -- I just whack the cup a couple of times gently on the edge of the trash can and 90% of the grounds fall out. A quick wipe with a damp paper towel gets rid of the rest, and after a quick rinse in the sink, the cup is ready to go again.

I haven't had mine long enough to assess durability, but I've seen nothing in the construction/manufacture that would lead me to believe that the cup is anything but well made. I'll update this if that opinion changes.
product: Breville BES870BSXL Barista Express Espresso Machine

text:
Purchased this in June 2011 when I received an estimate of $380 to fix the ice maker in my fridge. Eventually, I want to replace the fridge, but I'm not ready to do that just now, especially over an ice maker, especially since the rest of the fridge is working just fine!

Saw this on Amazon, and decided to go for it. I've had it for 16 months, and so far it works just fine. Occasionally I have to "reset" it by unplugging it, waiting a few seconds, then plugging it in and restarting it. The ice compartment is not chilled, so the ice tends to melt faster than you might expect, but once the ice level drops below a certain point, it starts making ice again.

And it is FAST. I use the "medium" size setting, and I'd say that within 10 minutes, I'm getting ice.

So far, I'm quite happy with it. I use it probably 3-4 times a week, maybe more in the summer (I live in the Pacific NW, and don't have air conditioning). No problems at all.

text:
I have been drinking "cold brew" for well over a decade now. The occasional coffee house would have a cold brew system, and so it was a treat back then to purchase a cup of extra strong and extra smooth coffee. Then I started making it at home. There were no special brew systems yet available. So I would simply take a big pitcher dump a bunch of ground coffee into it, then top it off with water. Cover, let sit for a full day, then either carefully pour into a cup as desired (while trying to keep grounds from falling out), or when I was feeling more motivated I would strain through a cheesecloth. Either way, I had easy cold brewed coffee. YUM.

Forward to the past several years where cold brew coffee has hit critical mass. Most everyone has heard of it, most coffee shops offer it, and many stores sell it. Many cold brew systems are available, just in different styles. Some coffee pot systems are trying to state that they offer cold brew option- but be aware-- cold brew is NOT ICED COFFEE. It is only cold/room temp water on ground coffee, there is no heat applied at any point (save for if you decide to heat it up after the fact). Ok, so.. this brewer:

I have tried a few different types of cold brew systems since my days of dumping loose ground coffee in a pitcher. These days things are a little more stream lined and a tad easier. None of them do anything extra special, it is really just about your preference. It basically breaks down to needing a filter of some sort (can be resuable metal cylinder or paper, like this one has), a vessel to "brew" in. That's it. This system happens to have a little extra in its design. I thought it might be a little much, but I found it pleasant to use, and there was no mess.

There appears to be many parts, but it is super easy to figure out. Instructions are super easy to understand if need be.

Steps:

--The brew container* (see additional info on this part below) is what what you will use to "brew" your coffee. The ratio is 1:8? 8 ounces (1/2 lb) of coffee to about 64 ounces of cold or room temp water. This does not need to be exact. You will not mess up a batch of cold brew if you use a little too much or a little less (though more would be better since you can water it down later if need be). You put course ground coffee into the included paper filter. If not already in base, then place coffee filled bag in side. Put cover on, and set aside on counter or in fridge for 12-24 hours. I usually wait a full day. Sometimes I go longer depending on when I started it. In this case, it was late afternoon, and I kept it on counter for a full day and a half so I could drink some in the morning.

*1. the brew vessel has a lever and stopper you will need to attach. Super quick and easy. Make sure the level is in the stop/closed position before adding water.
*2. Also, there is a 2 pack of carafe filters (small round sponges) that are optional use. I find they are easy to rinse and reuse.

--Once you are done "brewing", put the brewing vessel on top of the grooved pitcher. This is where the lever on the brewing vessel comes in. Push it open. This immediately drains your cold brew into a pourable pitcher. Once the brew vessel is empty, take out bag full of spent coffee and toss it out (OR if you are super thrifty and careful.. perhaps rinse and reuse?). Your pitcher is now full of delicious, smooth, concentrated coffee. The pitcher comes with a lid. The pitcher is of a workable size- easy to hold, small enough to store in fridge, yet holds nearly 2 liters. The size is perfect for storing in the fridge. Indents in the pitcher make it easy to grab/hold.

EDIT: Unlike other brew systems, it dawned on me that I could get another batch "brewing" at any time after the first one. This is because the brew container is separate from the storage container. My other "brewer" is a brewer and pitcher in one. So not until I finish the container (or find a different container to store concentrate in) can I make another batch. With this system, I can simply start another even if storage pitcher is in use (so long as I can access it it after brewing, which I tend to brew for 24-36 hours). Anyway, thought I would come back to add that in since it wasn't an immediate thought when I was using this initially.

"Cold brew" is considered a coffee concentrate. The idea is to have a strong coffee that can withstand being watered down by ice and other coffee additions (cream, syrups, etc) without losing the coffee taste. Or you can drink it straight if you like very strong coffee (I do!). In addition to that, it makes a very smooth coffee. Usually reduces/removes acidity. And lastly, although it seems like it is only consumed cold, it technically can be warmed up to be enjoyed hot. Some people cold brew to reduce the acidity, and then want to still have warm/hot coffee. Go for it. For me, I just really like the added oomph of taste that cold brew coffee makes. I have even noticed that it can make kinda blah coffee even better by being cold brewed. (have you ever purchased a container of coffee and not really liked it? Try cold brewing it-- it just might change things enough to make it palatable!).

Specially about this system: In short, I like it. I first thought it was going to be too many parts to deal with, but I see that the parts it comes with that once they are on, no need to take them off (save for if your brewer needs extra TLC when cleaning). The brew vessel AND a pitcher is more than what is really needed (often I see that a vessel could be the pitcher just by taking bag/filter out)- but this makes it super clean and easy to deal with. I DO like the paper filter over a metal filter in terms of speed of clean up, but some people would prefer not to have excess waste.

This style of system is really going to come down to preference. All the parts on mine look good, work soundly, and I have no problem with using this. With one caveat, the pitcher fits into the brew vessel for easy, compact storage (put lid on pitcher, but find spot for vessel lid). This system comes with TWO PLA bag filters, so you will need more. I do not know what this system costs but it doesn't need to be expensive to be effective. I see that the 25 pack of the filters is $15 (or less if buying 25 pack of regular filters). I have a glass with metal filter system that works well for around $25 although makes half as much at a time). Or just use my old school method of a pitcher with loose coffee in it. :)

If you choose this, know that it absolutely makes a great pitcher of cold brew coffee concentrate. No problems there.

Because I do not know what the price is, I can't automatically recommend this- but I would nudge people in the direction of this style. I personally think it is even easier than the metal filter style, and this makes a decent amount- not too much, but not too little. If it is within reason of other brewers, I would be real pleased. While I normally don't take into consideration price, I do if there are enough of similar products on market-- and knowing that cold brew systems are pretty much all the same except for filter material- that I can comfortably comment on it. Therefor I would normally ask, why would I recommend this over other brewers? That is when price might come into play, along with some other design features. Since this also requires use of paper filter, that is an additional cost. This just comes with two filters, so you would pretty much need to add in the additional cost right away. Say, if I was ordering this I would also order a pack of filters at same time.

What would improve upon product: While the pitcher and lid fits into the brewing vessel for more compact storage, the lid of the brewing vessel has nowhere to go. It could be placed on bottom so all of it just sits on it, or it can be placed on top but there again, it is just resting on it. It doesn't make for perfectly tidy storage. It would work if the brew vessel was taller or the pitcher was shorter-- then the pitcher would nestle down into the brew vessel and the lid could just go on top. The difference doesn't seem like it would be enough to displace water/coffee ratio, but would certainly add to the tidiness of storing it.

Current rating: 5 stars. It totally works as intended, and I have used enough cold brewers of various styles to be extra pleased with this one. I really didn't think it would be better than my simple glass pitcher with metal filter, but the paper filter and ease of separating the brewed coffee into a separate pitcher has impressed me. This system may be my new go-to brewer and I will pass my other one on to a friend. This is a matter of preference for me. Ultimately the rating goes toward the fact that it works well and produces a solid "cold brew" end result. I have been drinking a cup (ok, maybe 3) while writing this so excuse my length. I feel like writing a dissertation on cold brew while simultaneously running full speed on my treadmill. WEEEEEEEEEEe cold brew is fun! :)
product: Breville BES870BSXL Barista Express Espresso Machine

text:
"If You Care" products are pretty good. I buy them when I can as I like that they are all unbleached and chlorine free.

The #4 coffee filters work fine in a typical brew system or in use with a pour over style. I had used it in the latter and found that if I pressed the filter with my hand to squeeze out the last of the liquid from the coffee grounds, that it could tear along the seam. I don't think most people would encounter this as most people would use this for coffee to drip into using a machine. But just keep it in mind if you have a need to hand pour hot water over ground coffee- just let gravity do its job, and don't bother to help it along by squeezing out the rest.

It is compostable! After you make your coffee, toss it all into your compost pile!

They seem to make their products with care. Whenever possible I select one of their products over the typical beached or plastic laden product. I am not at all perfect in how I treat my environment, so even if I could do a lot better, I like knowing that t here are companies creating products that help us to have the "Least possible environmental impact" in our day to day lives.
product: Breville BES870BSXL Barista Express Espresso Machine

text:
[[VIDEOID:38607a72aabbe686d932e103c44a1d93]] THE GOOD:
--------------
* Quality components from the can housing to the cylinder, valve and fittings.
* Can smash taller 16 oz cans as well.
* Easy to assemble, even without instructions. All you do is look at the product photos and you'll get your answer of what goes where.
* Fun, fun, fun!
* Watch my video for some tips.

THE BAD:
------------
* No manual was in the box. So I asked the seller for it, and they sent me pictures quickly. But it was pointless. Because the "manual" was basically assembly images. I can figure out the assembly, just by looking at their final product photos. What I was looking for was tips, advice, explanations of why this fitting goes here, goes there, etc. Yet still, for a manual that was all about assembly, they leave out the most important part. How do you connect their fittings to an air compressor? Because their fitting is pretty unusual, at least to a newbie. In any case, I uploaded their manual photos for your convenience.
* I still don't know how they want us to connect their can crusher to an air compressor. They obviously have a method in mind, but they don't explain it. They included an extra elbow fitting, and something called an "air pump junction." It's the letter G item in the packet list photo in their listing page. Watch my video to understand my confusion.
* So I had to look at other can crushers on the market to understand how to connect this to an air compressor. What I found out was other sellers include a 1/4" NPT Quick Connect High Flow fitting. So that's what I got, and that's how I got this can crusher going. That's not good when your customers have to go to other sellers to see how things work. LOL!
* The valve included in this package does not automatically reset the cylinder disc. I've seen other can crushers where you push the button, it crushes the can. You let go, and the cylinder resets itself ready for the next crush. With this version, you press the button, it smashes the can, but the cylinder disc does not retract when you let go the button. You have to "Reset" by turning the button/knob clockwise. Then the disc retracts. Not a deal breaker, but just something worth mentioning.
* They are very stingy with the airline tubes. When you connect the airline tubes, you feel a CLICK. For the longer tube, I could not feel a click on both ends. I can feel a click on the end I start, but it wasn't long enough for me to get a good connection on the other end. So I ended up getting a lot of hissing, which means air leaking. So if you need more airline tube, then the search phrase you need to use is, "8mm pneumatic tubing." 8mm is what you want. The long tube that was in the package was 10.25", so I just cut myself something around 11". The shorter one was about 5", so I cut myself something around 6". If you're going to get into pneumatic tools, then these extra tubes you have to buy won't be a waste of money. They'll come in handy in the future.

In conclusion... If you are new to pneumatic can crushers, I do not recommend this listing. It was a lot of headache for a beginner like me. I'm good with tools, so I figure it all out. But if you want to avoid the cost of buying more tubes, and quick connect hose fittings, then I would look elsewhere. This was a 2-Star experience, but I hope that my review will help the seller improve on their product.
product: Breville BES870BSXL Barista Express Espresso Machine

text:
This is an excellent compact dishwasher that is perfect for a small space. It is about the size of a large microwave. I found that it is light enough for one person to maneuver, but it's easier with two people. It has a good capacity for such a compact unit. It's easy to operate and comes with a power cord and connection hoses. It's likely that you'll have to purchase an additional adapter to fit your faucet.

product: Instant Pot Duo 7-in-1 Electric Pressure Cooker

text:
I'm a decent sized guy and lift things around the house often but this things still about more than I can carry myself! I recommend using a dolly and then two people to hoist it up to where it's going. Then you need to run the drain hose to a sink drain or a big 5 gallon bucket would work (just make sure you have the hose secure or it could flop out and drain in the floor) Then you can hook this up to a sink or you can fill it's tank. I filled it's tank completely as I want to go that route and I loaded it up. Once the thing started running, I filled the tank all the way up again and it didn't have to be filled again for the whole wash cycle so it's pretty convenient. I think it'd be able to do everything on one full tank.

SO this is a smaller dishwasher and one level so you could fit one large pot and a couple cups maybe. But if you're doing little things- you can still fit a few things! It took it about an hour to run it's regular cycle and then it'd run it's fan for an hour drying it and pop open automatically when it's done. I just opened the door once it was done and let it all air dry though- no sense in having it dry things if you're not in a hurry.

Some things I love about this are: the UV light while drying (never seen that in a dishwasher!), it's very quiet- you could run it in an apartment at night without it being too loud, it cleans just as good as my full sized expensive $1100 dishwasher did! I had some dishes that hadn't been cleaned for a couple weeks because I was sick and I threw them in and they came out spotless! A couple pieces of silverware had a tiny bit of food residue but that happens with most dishwashers so no biggie- just sponge cleaned it off and rinsed it in seconds.

This thing would absolutely be great for an RV, tiny home, or just on the counter of a rental. On the rental- I'd check with the landlord first as some don't want you using a washer or dishwasher due to potential water leaks. But if it's allowed- get this thing, you will love it!! I truly never thought these mini dishwashers would clean so well but they do! Cleaning them is much easier than a full size too- it's super easy to pull the screen out and clean it!

No complaints or improvements that I can think of at all! It's heavy but it does the job so I'm fine with that!
product: Instant Pot Duo 7-in-1 Electric Pressure Cooker

text:
This makes the cutest little ice cubs, I think they're perfect if you are someone who eats ice. For an ice chest you'll probably be working at it for a while as this thing makes a handful of cubes every few minutes. The thing I love about this ice maker is it has a smaller profile than many of them I've seen. Many are much larger and bulkier. This one's not too bad at all. After using this thing a few times I can say that we love it! Seems to be well made and works perfectly.

The small ice and large ice are the exact same size, the large ice just stays on the metal rods longer so they become thicker. Both of them come out thin enough I can put them in my hand and just crush them completely. I honestly love this ice and am very happy with this!

To make Ice you just fill the bottom reservoir with water up to it's "Max" fill line which is marked "Max" and then it sucks water from that up to the top reservoir where the metal rods are cooled to the point that ice starts forming on them. Once the ice is ready it will drop the ice and the little motorized scoop will scoop it down into the little ice tray. Very easy to use and fun to watch!

text:
If you're searching for this, you know what it is. My gas range is the standard 36" range with built-in oven that is found in many homes. Nothing fancy. The problem is that there is a small gap between the range and the cabinet/countertop that food or liquid can fall into.

These Stainless Steel Stove Gap Covers solves exactly this problem. Due to its proximity to the fire and heat, the gap cover must be metal and because will be exposed to oils and other food particles, it needs to be easy-to-clean. These covers fit both criteria.

With an extendable piece, these covers can fit all ranges regardless of the depth of the stove and there is a small metal piece that is supposed to provide tension when inserted into the gap. Unfortunately, this is where I have the first problem with this product. The tension just isn't enough to keep the gap cover in the gap snugly. I think a cork or silicone piece would help give it the friction in needs to stay in place.

The second problem I have is that the product is in the shape of the letter "T" but because there is a slight difference in height between my range and the counter, it has to tilt to one side, further making it hard for the cover to stay in place.

The last issue is the extension part. It doesn't lock into a position. Without it, you can accidentally push on the cover and shorten it. It would be better to have some sort of locking mechanism that will keep it to the length that you set.

This product does what its intended for, but I think a few tweaks and it would be perfect (I might just look to see if I can make these tweaks myself).
product: Instant Pot Duo 7-in-1 Electric Pressure Cooker

text:
I've seen video reviews of products such as this BOSALY Ice Maker Machine. It promises to make ice in 9 minutes and I gotta say, it does just that. This ice maker can make two sizes of ice. In my testing, small ice cubes can be made in 9 minutes while the large one takes 11. I think it is necessary to set some expectations for this unit for those who are interested. If you know these things ahead of time, you will not be disappointed by your purchase:

This ice maker is fairly heavy, but that is to be expected because of the compressor. Also due to the compressor, this until will not work with power from a car adapter so as far as portability, you'd need to make sure there is a wall outlet or a generator that you can plug it into or you'll be draining a lot of battery power.

It must also be pointed out that the ice made by this machine is quite wet due to how the ice is made. It means that if you make ice with this machine, then put them into your freezer, you'll end up with one big lump of ice.

With that said, this machine does EXACTLY what I want it to do: make ice during a small gathering without the need for much oversight. Just make sure the water tank is filled.

Here are the features I like:
-- Portable. Be the savior of the party by bringing the ice maker for a friend's party.
-- Auto stop when the water level is low or the basket is filled.
-- Plug at the bottom allows draining of excess water.
-- FAST. 9 pieces of ice cube are made with each batch. That's enough for two cups.
-- Convenient. Make sure the machine is filled with water and plugged in. Come back once in a while to dump out the basket to make more ice or to refill the water.
-- Useful. This will definitely make a difference during a party. There are too many other things to worry about when preparing for guests.

Overall, great product.
product: Instant Pot Duo 7-in-1 Electric Pressure Cooker

text: I am always looking for a better way to monitor our extreme weather. So far this is working well. It seemed quite difficult to set up by following the instructions. Lots of things saying "from normal mode" do this and that, but no instructions or information on just what exactly normal mode is. It takes 6 AA batteries which are included. Large easy to read display with a light that is motion activated.  No hardware included for hanging the outside sensor, so right now mine is just laying in a dry place out of the sun whilst I figure out the best way to hang it. As for accuracy, it seems to be reading a degree or two higher than anything else I have for what it's worth. Seems well made.
product: Instant Pot Duo 7-in-1 Electric Pressure Cooker

text:
We recently moved to a new home in Florida. Prior to moving here I had decided to install a whole house dehumidifier. After installing it I wanted to be able to monitor the temperature and humidity levels throughout my house - I have done this using a few different methods, one of which is this set.

These simple small devices are easy to read and even easier to operate - just pull the tab and use.

My only complaint is 2 of the 10 in this set did not work out of the box. At this price it really is still reasonable for 8 units but not as great as for 10.

I have found these units work well, the batteries last a long time and the price is reasonable. While I wish all 10 of the units worked it still is a good set that I recommend.

text:
It's been 9 weeks since I've bought this item and its total piece of JUNK! As stated below I've has to replace on a yearly basis since orginal went out. But 9 weeks this is an absolute RIP OFF. I'm so pissed right now because i will have to blow dry until a better one arrives. EVEN WORSE I find out the reason it stopped working isnt because it blew out. I did buy a replacement again on amazon. Different seller and it's a different style.og element. The difference is the hood over top the element itself. They sell it for 4.95 and it is the same style I buy on line from a fridge company for 20$. No rather it last as long idk yet. But u you can read that review as well by clicking my name and viewing my older reviews. As always I will update and take serious my reviewing comments. Rank 4k and climbing for a reason. Good luck and be smart

Helpful hint do a google search for your element number and buy from someone other than amazon. These are not worth 2$ it's better to spend 20% and wait 5 days than this crap.. return window just closed 20 days ago which makes this even more of a sting.
DO NOT BUY I cant stress this enough DO NOT BUY

Works perfectly for my fridge.
I have seen alot of review talking about these not lasting very long. Heres my years of experience with this replacement element issues.
I bought my fridge used and really cheap because the people couldnt figure out how to fix it and thought it was trash.
Well the element was issue.
BUT
EVER since I replaced the element NO MATTER WHAT BRAND!! I have to replace once a year. Always in Aug because of the Alabama heat and humidity. My fridge runs really hard in summer months and ALWAYS blows out the element. So dont judge by those comments. I'm at year 5 now with the element issue and it isnt a brand thing i can promise you. It is just one of those things. This time around I replaced everything (included in this kit) let's see if that makes a difference. Idk though

The difference between all the others I've bought and this one is the guard over the element. The previous ones did not have the metal covering the top of element and that always made me wonder how it could last long with water dripping on a glowing red element. This one just may last a little longer because of that guard over the top only time will tell.

It seems well made, it is the right size thank God because the last time i bought, I could only find 8" ones but I got 2 for 22$. And even being 4" shorter they still lasted 1 year almost to the day. The price is about same I've paid in all years past as well. 2st time ordering on Amazon though but I 3xpext no changes and will buy a other one for a backup when this one does go out. I've learned its ALWAYS BEST to buy 2 at a time that way your not waiting for shipping and loosing meat. I always defr6with a blow drier and this has to be repeated every 2 days until replacement element arrives. It's a pain yes but better than smelling rotting food.

It is something we've gotten used too. It's either spend 20$ a year replacing the elements or Buying a new fridge @2k+. And I'm not down for that the thing works perfectly u til Aug rolls around..lol
This is the first time I've gotten the while kit. I've never replaced my thermostat or sensor. I think mine are working fine. But I went ahead and replaced them anyways just incase that's what may be contributing to my yearly replacement. Time will only tell and I will update as needed. I will be the 1st to report if it last longer or shorter than a year. But please only expect a year out of the replacement your lucky if you get more.

text: Quality OEM part that fixed the leaking on my Kenmore washer. I had to use WD-40 to get the old rusted part off the metal rod, and once I did, I had to buff off all the rust. If you don't buff the rust away, you risk cracking or breaking this part and then you're just right back to the same leaking problem. Some reviewers said this wasn't a quality part because that happened to them. That's not the manufacturer's fault that you broke it because of not cleaning off the rust. Pull up a youtube video which explains exactly how to replace it. Works like a charm for me! My wife is very please about no leaks now!

text: I like most things about this induction stove. The fact that I can cook two items on a stove. Top is very time, saving and very convenient. That said, when you’re using both of the burners at the same time, the energy output is split between the two burners, so both of them will not be able to be on highest heat at the same time. This means that if you want to boil water for pasta, for example, it will take almost 20 minutes to get your water to boil. And it will take about 20 minutes for your pasta to cook. Another thing I don’t like about the stove top is the fact that the cooking surface always looks dirty. No matter how much you clean it the circle that the pot rests on is slightly textured. And the surface never seems to come clean.
product: Instant Pot Duo 7-in-1 Electric Pressure Cooker

text:
The hood looks great & matches our other SS appliances well. It goes a long way to tying the room together. The fan is very quiet on low & acceptable on high. I thought my wife would object to the sound on high but she didn't think it was too much & we probably wouldn't run it on high all that much anyway.
We love the LED lights & use them a lot. They do a great job of illuminating the range and the spaces under the cabinets on either side. I do wish it had a dimmer knob or multiple setting for the LEDs. I mounted it at 30" above the counter which was about 28" above the range. It seemed high at first but, after 2 weeks in the kitchen, it is perfectly natural.
The stainless steel on the hood is very easy to wipe clean. The stainless on the chimney collected a lot of fingerprints during installation & is harder to clean. I don't anticipate the upper parts needing to be cleaned all that much but it was an observation.
The installation instructions are very good & I appreciate that. They provide charts for where the screws should go to achieve the desired height above the range instead of making the installer figure out the drops & all that. There are 2 places in the instructions that say "height above 36" High range" which was very confusing. I must have read through it a dozen times before I came to the conclusion that it probably meant '36" WIDE range'.
This is the first time I have installed one of these & I expected it to be a big job. We renovated the kitchen in this house before we moved in & the only thought I had to the vent hood was to drop an electrical outlet in the space. The hood needs framing behind the drywall to mount, everything else can be hung with the included drywall hangers. I had to move my outlet from the center to the side, drop in some wood behind the drywall & I also modified the mounting plate to connect directly to a stud that was off-center. It looks rough but it's all hidden behind the chimney now. Simply replacing an old vent probably wouldn't involve all of that.
Final mounting & assembly with the connected pipe & chimney pieces was tricky for me. Now that I have done it, I would do it in a different order than the instructions stated but an experienced installer probably already knows that.
The hood arrived very well packed & with a thick plastic wrap around the stainless parts. That kind of detail & the good instructions leads me to believe this is going to be a quality product.
product: Breville BES870BSXL Barista Express Espresso Machine

text: Our other portable washer finally bit the dust (the water pump to discharge the water) after using it hard for four years. I was going to just buy another but the price had jumped to double and I couldn't see it as being a logical thing to do so I investigated all the portable washers out there and decided to buy this one. I'm not sorry I did. It holds the same amount as my RCA washer did yet is much smaller, quieter, and faster. The only thing I'm confused about is I'm not sure how to operate it..I'm just guessing right now as I am going blind and can't read the small print on the booklet that came with it. I'm trying to find an online version so I can enlarge it with my computer. Other than that it's a great little machine at a good price. I highly recommend it! Edit: Got it figured out on my own and still working great! Who needs a thousand dollar washing machine!!!! This works well for hubby and me just fine! We even nicknamed her "Lol bit" 👍😁💖

text: This is a great way to secure a standard door refrigerator. I volunteer for a food distribution pantry that often stores items in a fridge for later. The mechanism isn't perfect, I've definitely used easier and smoother combination locks but I love how easy this is to install. You can use adhesive or screws (both included), but it will not work well on French Door style fridges. For what we needed, it works well so far!

text:
I like the size of this and the fact that it doesn't require filters. This is good for travelling and even camping, since it is relatively easy to use and clean and doesn't require much else to work. The mesh is good about keeping the grounds out of your coffee, however I did notice a little more at the bottom of my cup than I do with a paper filter. Perhaps I need to try a more coarse grind, as fine grounds are getting through a little.
What makes it so petite are the arms that fold up for storage and then fold out to hold itself over the cup. The only issue with this, however, is that the arms hold the filter partially submerged, so you can never fill the cup to the top. If using with a tall mug, this is fine, especially if you leave room for milk. But with a standard 8 or 10 oz disposable cup, you might find that you can't get enough coffee out of it at a time as the filter sits about 1/4 of the cup down.
It definitely has its uses, but know the limitations before you get it.
product: Breville BES870BSXL Barista Express Espresso Machine

text:

This is a great tool to monitor how things are doing in different rooms in the house despite the house thermostat reading. The humidity is really useful to know as well. I've learned a lot about my house just leaving this in different rooms for a day or two at a time.
It is easy to use thanks to a touchscreen, and the backlight is a nice feature but not entirely necessary. I like that it comes with batteries so everything you need is in the box already. I've always wanted one of these after seeing them in other homes, and it is everything I wanted it to be. Very happy with it.

text:
We have an old Caloric brand wall oven that came with the house. I have no idea how old it is, but within a year or two of living here, the oven stopped working. It would glow, but no gas would come out and it would never start. I called an appliance repair man who determined it was a bad ignitor. He explained to me that the gas won't turn on until the ignitor reaches a certain temp, so either the ignitor wasn't getting hot enough, or it wasn't sending the signal to turn on the gas when it did. Either way, the problem was entirely with the ignitor. He replaced it, and something like $200 later, the oven was back to working. Until it stopped working again about a year after that. This time it didn't even glow, the oven would just do nothing.

Instead of another expensive repair, I decided on a hunch to assume the part the repair man brought was lower quality than the original. I looked it up online and found a match here on Amazon. I felt confident that I could swap it myself now that I saw the repair guy do it, and sure enough this did the trick for a fraction of what I would pay him to come back! Its been a little over a month since putting it in, and so far so good. I'm hoping this will last longer than the last one, but even if it doesn't, worst case scenario is that I'll spend $20-30 on an annual repair until we can afford to replace the whole oven! I can deal with that.
product: Breville BES870BSXL Barista Express Espresso Machine

text:
Okay, I read all the reviews & I still bought it.

I have the B60 Keurig maker & I love it! I use it every day, several times a day. I have no time in the morning to brew a whole pot of coffee & when I get home at night, I'm not in the mood for the fuss of cleaning out a pot. So this was my solution.

I did hesitate on buying it being I am a huge Dunkin Donuts fan. I live on their coffee! I couldn't see switching brands just to buy the Keurig, but I did once I saw they sold this special filter.

I received the filter today & went right into the kitchen to put it to work. First off, removing the Keurig filter was easy. The filter came right out. The reuseable filter slipped right in & I didn't have any problems. Granted this review is based on my first use, but to be honest, I think it should last if I take care of it.

I'm going to use it again when I'm in the mood for my own coffee versus the K cup.

For the record - Timothy's Winter Carnival is the BEST!

text: This is the 5th one I've bought - all the previous ones were nicked by my friends or I gave them away. Look, good coffee anywhere is not difficult. You pack this, and some filters in your kit. If you are going somewhere with good coffee, get it there, if not, put some nice ground coffee in a bag and add that to your kit. If you will be somewhere with mugs, use those, if not, add a mug to your kit. So if you will be on a ship crossing the Pacific with only instant coffee on board for 4 weeks you pack this doohicky, about 30 filters, enough ground coffee for 30 days, your favorite mug, and you're set! Easier to clean than a french press, far less breakable too.  I just added a kit to my tiny travel trailer. Just say NO to coffee pods!

text: My dryer started squeaking and squealing like a stuck pig--and a quick google search revealed it was probably the "supports" and these parts were listed.  I only spent $40 on the dryer 6 years ago (used), so I was hoping the repair wouldn't be too costly. Well, this kit doubled the value of my dryer!  Turns out three of the four supports were worn to oblivion.  My dad came up and the two of us were able to make the repair fairly easily (it was work, don't get me wrong, but other than trying to get the belt around the pulley, it wasn't HARD work, just a little effort.) Way better than having to pay someone to do it!  We replaced all four supports, the arch, foam and the belt (which wasn't worn, but figured since we had it open might as well).  Here's hoping to another 6+ years!  The parts fit perfectly and it was pretty easy to figure out with the diagrams and a little smarts.

text:
The Binazon LT120F air filters arrived in a small box which each filter individually wrapped. Upon inspection, I found no issues with any of them. I removed the existing air filter which was an OEM filter and refrigerator and compared them. The only differences were color tint and the Binazon had more tape, one piece per side while OEM only had two pieces. The Binazon is actually a better quality fair filter in my opinion.

Measurements are close to those stated in the ad. The difference is not enough to matter about 1/16”. The Binazon appear to be made from the same material as OEM…plastic. The Binazon filter fit right into the mount and was easliy installed with no issues.

These are a great buy for the price and overall quality. I would recommend to others.

text: The first one of these I bought was a Cuisinart. Loved it but we wore it out. My sister-in-law waxed poetic about her Keurig machine so we bought one after our old Cuisinart broke beyond repair. I hated it. It was louder and clunky and the "make your own" basket had to be purchased separately and never quite worked right in the machine. When our coffee started tasting weird, that was the final straw--I chucked the Keurig and bought this one. It's a less expensive version of our first Cuisinart but it is so much better than the Keurig. It's quieter, the "make your own" basket comes with the unit (and even has it's own storage drawer for when not in use), and it's so easy to use.
